text: A 64-year-old man has progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, bilateral crackles, and pitting leg edema. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: CHF: dyspnea + orthopnea + bilateral crackles + peripheral edema + elevated BNP. Tx: furosemide + ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker.

text: Which medication reduces mortality in systolic heart failure?
rationale: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality by decreasing afterload and preventing cardiac remodeling. Diuretics relieve symptoms only. Beta-blockers and spironolactone also reduce mortality.

text: A 58-year-old man has chest pain only with exertion, fully relieved by rest within 5 minutes. ECG normal at rest. Diagnosis?
rationale: Stable angina: predictable exertional pain relieved by rest or nitrates. Fixed coronary stenosis. Dx: stress test. Tx: beta-blocker + nitrates + aspirin + statin.

text: A 70-year-old has chest pain at rest, lasts 20 minutes, and troponin is elevated. ECG shows ST depression. Diagnosis?
rationale: NSTEMI: chest pain at rest + elevated troponin + ST depression (no ST elevation). Tx: aspirin + heparin + beta-blocker + early PCI within 24-72 hours.

text: A 70-year-old smoker has hemoptysis, weight loss, and a spiculated lung mass. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Lung cancer: smoker + hemoptysis + weight loss + spiculated mass. Adenocarcinoma is #1 overall. Small cell: most aggressive, paraneoplastic syndromes.

text: A 65-year-old smoker has chronic productive cough for 3+ years and frequent respiratory infections. FEV1/FVC < 0.70. Diagnosis?
rationale: COPD: smoking history + chronic productive cough + obstructive pattern (FEV1/FVC <0.70). GOLD staging by FEV1. Tx: bronchodilators + smoking cessation.

text: A 25-year-old woman has episodic wheezing, chest tightness, and dyspnea that improves with bronchodilators. Diagnosis?
rationale: Asthma: reversible airflow obstruction + episodic wheezing + responds to bronchodilators. Dx: spirometry with bronchodilator response. Tx: inhaled corticosteroids + SABA as needed.

text: A 30-year-old tall thin man has sudden onset pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. CXR shows absent lung markings at apex. Diagnosis?
rationale: Spontaneous pneumothorax: tall thin young male + sudden pleuritic pain + dyspnea + absent breath sounds + absent lung markings on CXR. Tx: oxygen; chest tube if large.

text: A 50-year-old alcoholic has epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea, vomiting. Lipase 4x upper limit. Best initial treatment?
rationale: Pancreatitis: aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the most important initial treatment. NPO + analgesia. Antibiotics only if infected necrosis.

text: A 55-year-old man has painless progressive jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and 12 kg weight loss. Diagnosis?
rationale: Painless jaundice + weight loss in elderly = pancreatic head cancer. Courvoisier sign: palpable non-tender gallbladder. Dx: CT abdomen. CA 19-9 elevated.

text: A 45-year-old man has heartburn, regurgitation, and dysphagia to solids. Endoscopy shows columnar metaplasia in distal esophagus. Diagnosis?
rationale: Barrett esophagus: columnar metaplasia replacing squamous epithelium in distal esophagus due to chronic GERD. Precancerous lesion. Regular surveillance endoscopy required.

text: A 60-year-old man has rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, and 8 kg weight loss. Colonoscopy shows mass in sigmoid colon. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Colorectal cancer: rectal bleeding + change in bowel habits + weight loss in older adult. Screening colonoscopy at age 50. Tx: surgical resection + adjuvant chemo for stage III.

text: A 25-year-old woman has relapsing-remitting neurologic deficits: visual loss, then leg weakness months later. MRI shows periventricular white matter lesions. Diagnosis?
rationale: MS: young woman + relapsing-remitting course + disseminated CNS lesions on MRI (time and space). Optic neuritis common first presentation. Tx: disease-modifying therapy (interferon-beta).

text: A 70-year-old man has resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and shuffling gait. Diagnosis?
rationale: Parkinson disease: resting tremor + rigidity + bradykinesia + postural instability (TRAP). Loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra. Tx: Levodopa/carbidopa.

text: A 65-year-old woman has progressive memory loss, disorientation, and personality changes over 2 years. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Alzheimer disease: #1 cause of dementia. Gradual memory loss → disorientation → personality changes. Amyloid plaques + neurofibrillary tangles. Tx: cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil).

text: A 30-year-old woman has fatigue, pallor, and glossitis. CBC shows microcytic hypochromic anemia. Serum ferritin is low. Diagnosis?
rationale: Iron deficiency anemia: microcytic hypochromic + low ferritin + low serum iron + high TIBC. Causes: poor intake, blood loss, malabsorption. Tx: oral iron supplements.

text: A 55-year-old has megaloblastic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, and subacute combined degeneration. Diagnosis?
rationale: B12 deficiency: megaloblastic anemia + neurologic symptoms (subacute combined degeneration). Folate deficiency: megaloblastic anemia WITHOUT neurologic symptoms. Dx: serum B12. Tx: IM cyanocobalamin.

text: A 60-year-old man has leukocytosis, splenomegaly, and Philadelphia chromosome (BCR-ABL) positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: CML: Philadelphia chromosome (t9;22) BCR-ABL = pathognomonic. Leukocytosis + splenomegaly. Tx: Imatinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) = first-line.

text: A 25-year-old woman has severe dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. Pelvic exam shows tender nodules in posterior fornix. Diagnosis?
rationale: Endometriosis: ectopic endometrial tissue outside uterus. Classic triad: dysmenorrhea + dyspareunia + infertility. Posterior fornix nodules = classic finding. Dx: laparoscopy (gold standard).

text: A 35-year-old woman has heavy menstrual bleeding and a firm enlarged uterus. Pelvic ultrasound shows multiple intramural masses. Diagnosis?
rationale: Uterine fibroids: most common benign uterine tumor. Heavy bleeding + enlarged firm uterus + multiple masses on US. Estrogen-dependent. Tx: GnRH agonist, myomectomy, or hysterectomy.

text: A 28-year-old woman at 10 weeks gestation has vaginal bleeding and crampy lower abdominal pain. Cervix is closed on exam. Diagnosis?
rationale: Threatened abortion: vaginal bleeding + cramping + CLOSED cervix + pregnancy still viable. Tx: pelvic rest, avoid intercourse, serial beta-hCG and ultrasound monitoring.

text: A 25-year-old woman has 6 weeks amenorrhea, vaginal bleeding, and severe right lower quadrant pain. Beta-hCG is positive. Transvaginal ultrasound shows no intrauterine pregnancy. Diagnosis?
rationale: Ectopic pregnancy: positive hCG + no IUP on TVUS + pelvic pain = ectopic until proven otherwise. Most common site: fallopian tube. Tx: methotrexate (if stable) or surgery.

text: A 70-year-old man has a 6 cm abdominal aortic aneurysm found incidentally. He is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate management?
rationale: AAA ≥5.5 cm = elective repair indicated. AAA <5.5 cm = surveillance ultrasound every 6-12 months. Emergency surgery = ruptured AAA (hypotension + pulsatile mass).

text: An 80-year-old woman falls and has pain in her right hip. She cannot bear weight. X-ray shows displaced femoral neck fracture. Best treatment?
rationale: Displaced femoral neck fracture in elderly = total hip arthroplasty. Internal fixation has high non-union rates in elderly. Early mobilization essential to prevent complications.

text: A 25-year-old man has knee pain and locking after a twisting injury playing football. MRI shows medial meniscus tear. Initial management?
rationale: Meniscus tear: initial conservative management = RICE + physiotherapy. Arthroscopic surgery if symptoms persist or knee locked. Young athletes with bucket-handle tear: early surgery.

text: A 22-year-old man has auditory hallucinations, paranoid delusions, disorganized speech, and social withdrawal for 8 months. Diagnosis?
rationale: Schizophrenia: positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) + negative symptoms + >6 months. Tx: atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine). Clozapine for treatment-resistant.

text: A 30-year-old man has episodes of elevated mood, decreased sleep, grandiosity, and increased energy lasting 7 days, alternating with depression. Diagnosis?
rationale: Bipolar I: full manic episodes (≥7 days or hospitalized) alternating with depression. Bipolar II: hypomania + depression (no full mania). Tx: lithium or valproate as mood stabilizer.

text: A 35-year-old woman with bipolar disorder is pregnant. Which mood stabilizer is absolutely contraindicated in first trimester?
rationale: Valproate: absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy - causes neural tube defects (spina bifida) in 1-2%. Also: facial dysmorphia, cognitive impairment. Lithium: Ebstein anomaly risk (small). Lamotrigine: safest option.

text: A 45-year-old alcoholic has tremors, sweating, and seizures 48 hours after stopping alcohol. Diagnosis?
rationale: Alcohol withdrawal: tremors + autonomic instability + seizures at 6-48 hours. Delirium tremens at 48-72 hours = most severe (confusion + hallucinations). Tx: benzodiazepines (lorazepam/diazepam).

text: A 50-year-old chronic alcoholic has confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia (Wernicke triad). What is the immediate treatment?
rationale: Wernicke encephalopathy: give thiamine IV BEFORE glucose (glucose depletes thiamine → precipitates Wernicke). Classic triad: confusion + ataxia + ophthalmoplegia. Korsakoff = chronic amnesia if untreated.

text: A newborn at 32 weeks gestation develops respiratory distress within hours of birth. CXR shows ground-glass appearance. Diagnosis?
rationale: RDS (Hyaline membrane disease): premature infant + surfactant deficiency → ground glass CXR. Tx: exogenous surfactant + CPAP/ventilation. Prevention: antenatal corticosteroids to mother.

text: A newborn has jaundice at 2 hours of life. Maternal blood type is O+, baby is A+. Direct Coombs test is positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: ABO incompatibility: jaundice within 24 hours + positive direct Coombs + mother O / baby A or B. Tx: phototherapy; exchange transfusion if severe. Physiologic jaundice: appears day 2-3.

text: A 3-year-old child has no meaningful speech, avoids eye contact, engages in repetitive hand-flapping, and has difficulty with transitions. Diagnosis?
rationale: Autism spectrum disorder: impaired social communication + restricted repetitive behaviors. Red flags: no babbling by 12 months, no single words by 16 months, no 2-word phrases by 24 months. Screen with M-CHAT.

text: A 7-year-old boy has polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss for 2 weeks. Blood glucose 25 mmol/L. pH 7.20. HCO3 12. Diagnosis?
rationale: DKA in T1DM: polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss + hyperglycemia + metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3 + HCO3 <15). Tx: IV fluids first, then insulin infusion + electrolyte replacement.

text: A 25-year-old man has a targetoid skin lesion (target lesion) with central blister following herpes simplex infection. Diagnosis?
rationale: Erythema multiforme: target lesions (3 zones: central blister + pale ring + erythematous halo). Common trigger: HSV infection. Minor = self-limiting. Major = mucous membrane involvement.

text: A 60-year-old man has a pearly, rolled-edge skin lesion on his nose with telangiectasia. He is a farmer with heavy sun exposure. Diagnosis?
rationale: Basal cell carcinoma: most common skin cancer. Pearly rolled edge + telangiectasia + sun-exposed areas. Very rarely metastasizes. Tx: surgical excision or Mohs surgery.

text: A 70-year-old woman has an asymmetric pigmented skin lesion with irregular borders and multiple colors on her back. Diagnosis?
rationale: Melanoma: ABCDE criteria = Asymmetry + Border irregularity + Color variation + Diameter >6mm + Evolution. Most dangerous skin cancer. Dx: excisional biopsy. Staging determines treatment.

text: A 60-year-old diabetic has eGFR 25 mL/min/1.73m², proteinuria 3.5g/day, and bilateral small kidneys on ultrasound. Diagnosis?
rationale: CKD stage 4: eGFR 15-29 + small kidneys (irreversible). Diabetic nephropathy = #1 cause of CKD. Tx: RAAS blockade + SGLT2 inhibitor. Prepare for dialysis if eGFR <15.

text: A 25-year-old man has periorbital edema, massive proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminemia, and hyperlipidemia. Diagnosis?
rationale: Nephrotic syndrome: massive proteinuria (>3.5g/day) + hypoalbuminemia + edema + hyperlipidemia. Children: minimal change disease #1. Adults: focal segmental glomerulosclerosis #1. Tx: steroids.

text: A 30-year-old man has hematuria, hypertension, oliguria, and RBC casts in urine after a strep throat infection. Diagnosis?
rationale: Nephritic syndrome: hematuria + hypertension + oliguria + RBC casts = glomerulonephritis. Post-strep GN: 2-3 weeks after strep throat. Usually self-limiting in children.

text: A 45-year-old woman has fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, and dry skin. TSH is elevated, free T4 is low. Diagnosis?
rationale: Hypothyroidism: elevated TSH + low T4. Most common cause: Hashimoto thyroiditis (autoimmune). Tx: levothyroxine (T4 replacement). Monitor TSH 6-8 weeks after dose adjustment.

text: A 50-year-old woman has weakness, hyperpigmentation of skin and gums, hypotension, and hyponatremia. Cortisol is low. Diagnosis?
rationale: Addison disease: hyperpigmentation + hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + low cortisol. Cause: autoimmune adrenal destruction. Addisonian crisis: life-threatening. Tx: hydrocortisone.

text: A 30-year-old woman has butterfly malar rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, and proteinuria. ANA and anti-dsDNA antibodies are positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: SLE: malar rash + photosensitivity + arthritis + nephritis + positive ANA/anti-dsDNA. Anti-dsDNA most specific. Anti-Smith also specific. Tx: hydroxychloroquine (first-line).

text: A 65-year-old woman has temporal headache, jaw claudication, and sudden visual loss. ESR is 95 mm/hr. Diagnosis?
rationale: Giant cell arteritis: temporal headache + jaw claudication + visual loss in elderly. ESR >50. Emergency: give HIGH-DOSE steroids immediately to prevent permanent blindness. Dx: temporal artery biopsy.

text: A 25-year-old woman has dysuria, frequency, and suprapubic pain. Urine culture grows E. coli. No fever or flank pain. Diagnosis?
rationale: Uncomplicated UTI: dysuria + frequency + suprapubic pain + no systemic features. E. coli = #1 cause (80%). Tx: nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole x3-5 days.

text: A 35-year-old woman has fever, flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, and pyuria. Urine culture grows E. coli. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pyelonephritis: UTI symptoms + fever + flank pain + CVA tenderness + pyuria/bacteriuria. Tx: fluoroquinolones or cephalosporins x7-14 days. IV antibiotics if septic.

text: A 28-year-old man has a painless penile ulcer with indurated edges and non-tender inguinal lymphadenopathy. VDRL is positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: Primary syphilis: painless indurated ulcer (chancre) + non-tender lymphadenopathy. Caused by Treponema pallidum. Dx: dark-field microscopy or VDRL/RPR + FTA-ABS. Tx: benzathine penicillin G.

text: A competent 55-year-old Jehovah's Witness refuses blood transfusion needed for surgery. He understands the risk. Most appropriate action?
rationale: Competent adult has absolute right to refuse treatment including life-saving therapy. Document capacity. Explore alternatives (cell salvage, erythropoietin). Family CANNOT override capable adult.

text: A physician makes a medication error causing no harm. What is the obligation?
options: No disclosure needed if no harm
rationale: Full disclosure of adverse events and near-misses is mandatory in Canada even without harm. Apology is not admission of liability (Ontario Apology Act). Transparency builds trust and is legally required.

text: A 70-year-old with terminal cancer has a valid Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) order. He arrests in hospital. What should the team do?
options: Provide comfort care only - no CPR
Attempt CPR while checking with family
Start CPR then stop if family agrees
rationale: Valid DNR order must be respected. Do NOT attempt CPR. Provide comfort/palliative care. DNR does not mean do not treat - continue symptom management. Review orders regularly with patient and family.
